Real analysis

Undergraduate analysis on $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{R}^n$: completeness, continuity, differentiation, Riemann integration, uniform convergence, and the inverse/implicit function theorems. Metric spaces and general topology live on point-set topology; Lebesgue measure and $L^p$ spaces are the subject of measure theory.

blocks.html:
1. Real numbers and completeness

Calculus textbooks write $\mathbb{R}$ as "the number line" and move on. Analysis begins by asking what property of $\mathbb{R}$, beyond $\mathbb{Q}$, actually does the work. The answer is the least-upper-bound property: every nonempty subset $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ that is bounded above has a smallest upper bound, $\sup S\in\mathbb{R}$. Equivalently — by Dedekind cuts, by Cauchy sequences, or by nested intervals — $\mathbb{R}$ is a complete ordered field, and that word "complete" is what fails for $\mathbb{Q}$.

Why $\mathbb{Q}$ isn't enough. Consider $S=\{q\in\mathbb{Q}: q>0, q^2<2\}\subset\mathbb{Q}$. This set is nonempty and bounded above (by $2$), yet it has no least upper bound in $\mathbb{Q}$: for any rational $q$ with $q^2\ge 2$, one can always find a smaller rational $q'\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $q'>\sqrt 2$. In $\mathbb{R}$ the supremum exists and equals $\sqrt 2\notin\mathbb{Q}$. The rationals have holes exactly where irrational cuts ought to live.

Three equivalent forms of completeness

  • Least upper bound (LUB): every nonempty bounded-above $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ has $\sup S\in\mathbb{R}$.
  • Cauchy completeness: every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges. (A sequence $(a_n)$ is Cauchy if $\forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists N\;\forall m,n\ge N: |a_m-a_n|<\varepsilon$.)
  • Nested intervals: if $[a_1,b_1]\supseteq [a_2,b_2]\supseteq\cdots$ is a nested sequence of closed bounded intervals with $b_n-a_n\to 0$, then $\bigcap_n [a_n,b_n]$ is a single point.

Over an ordered Archimedean field these are logically interchangeable; losing any one forces one to lose all three. Proofs of the big theorems (intermediate value, extreme value, Heine–Borel on intervals) all reduce to completeness.

Dedekind's construction

A Dedekind cut is a partition $(L,U)$ of $\mathbb{Q}$ with $L$ nonempty, bounded above, downward-closed, and with no maximum. Each cut names a real number: the rationals less than it. Addition, multiplication, and order lift from $\mathbb{Q}$. Cauchy's alternative construction builds $\mathbb{R}$ as equivalence classes of Cauchy rational sequences. Both constructions produce the same — unique up to ordered-field isomorphism — complete ordered field.

Rational approximations to $\sqrt 2$

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#sqrt2-svg'), out=$('#sqrt2-out'), slider=$('#sqrt2-q'), qpill=$('#sqrt2-qval');
  const W=760, H=210, pad=40;
  const xmin=1.25, xmax=1.55;
  function X(v){ return pad + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-2*pad); }
  function bestRational(maxQ){
    // best rational p/q with q<=maxQ and (p/q)^2 < 2, and smallest upper (p/q)^2 > 2
    let bestLo={p:1,q:1,val:1}, bestHi={p:2,q:1,val:2};
    for(let q=1;q<=maxQ;q++){
      // p around sqrt(2)*q
      const pc = Math.floor(Math.SQRT2 * q);
      for(const p of [pc, pc+1]){
        if(p<=0) continue;
        const v=p/q, sq=v*v;
        if(sq<2 && v>bestLo.val){ bestLo={p,q,val:v}; }
        if(sq>2 && v<bestHi.val){ bestHi={p,q,val:v}; }
      }
    }
    return {lo:bestLo, hi:bestHi};
  }
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const maxQ = +slider.value;
    qpill.textContent = `q ≤ ${maxQ}`;
    // axis
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pad,y1:H/2,x2:W-pad,y2:H/2,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1.4}));
    // tick marks
    for(let t=1.3;t<=1.5;t+=0.05){
      const tx=X(t);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:tx,y1:H/2-5,x2:tx,y2:H/2+5,stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1}));
      const lab=SVG('text',{x:tx,y:H/2+22,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lab.textContent = t.toFixed(2); svg.appendChild(lab);
    }
    // √2 marker
    const sx = X(Math.SQRT2);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:sx,y1:30,x2:sx,y2:H-50,stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'4 4'}));
    const lab=SVG('text',{x:sx,y:22,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:'var(--yellow)','font-weight':600});
    lab.textContent='√2 ≈ 1.41421…'; svg.appendChild(lab);
    // best rationals
    const {lo, hi} = bestRational(maxQ);
    const drawDot=(r,color,label,dy)=>{
      const rx = X(r.val);
      if(rx < pad || rx > W-pad) return;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:rx,cy:H/2,r:5,fill:color,stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.5}));
      const t1=SVG('text',{x:rx,y:H/2+dy,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:color,'font-weight':600});
      t1.textContent = `${r.p}/${r.q}`; svg.appendChild(t1);
      const t2=SVG('text',{x:rx,y:H/2+dy+14,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t2.textContent = r.val.toFixed(5); svg.appendChild(t2);
    };
    drawDot(lo,'var(--blue)','lo', -40);
    drawDot(hi,'var(--pink)','hi', -40);
    // readout
    out.textContent =
      `best rational lower bound  :  ${lo.p}/${lo.q} = ${lo.val.toFixed(8)}   (square = ${(lo.val*lo.val).toFixed(6)})\n`+
      `best rational upper bound  :  ${hi.p}/${hi.q} = ${hi.val.toFixed(8)}   (square = ${(hi.val*hi.val).toFixed(6)})\n`+
      `gap                        :  ${(hi.val-lo.val).toExponential(3)}   — shrinks to 0, but never snaps to a rational`;
  }
  slider.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
As $q$ grows, rational approximations squeeze in on $\sqrt 2$ from both sides, yet neither side ever reaches a rational equal to $\sqrt 2$. The existence of the supremum is therefore not automatic — it is the additional axiom that distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$.

Archimedean property. For every real $x$ there is a natural number $n$ with $n>x$. This sounds obvious and follows from completeness: if $\mathbb{N}$ were bounded above, it would have a supremum $s$, but $s-1$ would also be an upper bound. The Archimedean property excludes infinitesimals and is what lets $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ arguments grind forward.

2. Continuity on $\mathbb{R}$

A function $f\colon D\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous at $a\in D$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $\delta>0$ such that

$$ |x-a|<\delta \;\Longrightarrow\; |f(x)-f(a)|<\varepsilon. $$

The definition trades a target tolerance $\varepsilon$ (how close the output must be to $f(a)$) for a domain tolerance $\delta$ (how close the input must be to $a$). The function is continuous on $D$ if it is continuous at every point of $D$.

$\varepsilon$–$\delta$ on $f(x)=x^2$ at $a=1$
shrink $\delta$ until the $x$-box lands inside the $\varepsilon$-box
ε = 0.40 δ = 0.30

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#eps-svg'), out=$('#eps-out');
  const eE=$('#eps-e'), eD=$('#eps-d'), eLab=$('#eps-eval'), dLab=$('#eps-dval');
  const W=560, H=340, pl=50, pr=20, pt=20, pb=40;
  const xmin=-0.4, xmax=2.4, ymin=-0.3, ymax=5.2;
  function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
  const a=1;
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const eps=+eE.value, d=+eD.value;
    eLab.textContent = `ε = ${eps.toFixed(2)}`;
    dLab.textContent = `δ = ${d.toFixed(2)}`;
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:pt,x2:X(0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    // epsilon band (horizontal around f(a)=1)
    const fa = a*a;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:pl, y:Y(fa+eps), width:W-pl-pr, height:Y(fa-eps)-Y(fa+eps),
      fill:'rgba(255,216,102,0.11)', stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4','stroke-width':1}));
    // delta band (vertical around x=a)
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:X(a-d), y:pt, width:X(a+d)-X(a-d), height:H-pt-pb,
      fill:'rgba(88,196,221,0.10)', stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4','stroke-width':1}));
    // f(x) = x^2
    const pts=[]; const N=220;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      pts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(x*x)}`);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    // point (a, f(a))
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(a),cy:Y(fa),r:4,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.2}));
    // labels
    const la=SVG('text',{x:X(a)+6,y:Y(fa)-8,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--yellow)'});
    la.textContent='(1, 1)'; svg.appendChild(la);
    // check: does f([a-d, a+d]) ⊂ (f(a)-eps, f(a)+eps) ?
    // For f=x^2 on neighborhood of 1, range is ((a-d)^2, (a+d)^2) when a-d>0
    let lo, hi;
    if(a-d>=0){ lo=(a-d)*(a-d); hi=(a+d)*(a+d); }
    else { lo=0; hi=Math.max((a-d)*(a-d),(a+d)*(a+d)); }
    const ok = (lo >= fa-eps-1e-12) && (hi <= fa+eps+1e-12);
    const maxDev = Math.max(Math.abs(hi-fa), Math.abs(fa-lo));
    out.textContent =
      `f(x) = x² ,  a = 1 ,  f(a) = 1\n`+
      `f maps [${(a-d).toFixed(2)}, ${(a+d).toFixed(2)}] onto [${lo.toFixed(3)}, ${hi.toFixed(3)}]\n`+
      `we need this ⊆ [${(fa-eps).toFixed(3)}, ${(fa+eps).toFixed(3)}]  (half-width ε = ${eps.toFixed(2)})\n`+
      `max deviation from f(a): ${maxDev.toFixed(3)}\n\n`+
      (ok
        ? `✓ ε–δ satisfied: |x-1|<δ  ⇒  |x²-1| ≤ ${maxDev.toFixed(3)} < ε`
        : `✗ δ too big: some x with |x-1|<δ has |x²-1| = ${maxDev.toFixed(3)} > ε.  Shrink δ.`);
  }
  eE.addEventListener('input',render);
  eD.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Two theorems that live and die with completeness

Intermediate value theorem (IVT). If $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $y$ lies between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists $c\in[a,b]$ with $f(c)=y$. The proof is a bisection argument: repeatedly halve the interval, keeping the half where $f$ changes sign relative to $y$. Completeness delivers the limit point. Without completeness, the continuous function $f(q)=q^2-2$ on $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,2]$ would have $f(0)=-2$, $f(2)=2$, but no rational zero.

Extreme value theorem (EVT). A continuous function on a closed bounded interval $[a,b]$ attains its maximum and minimum. This follows from the Heine–Borel fact that $[a,b]$ is compact (every open cover admits a finite subcover) plus the observation that continuous images of compact sets are compact, hence bounded and closed — in $\mathbb{R}$, that means they contain their sup and inf.

Uniform continuity. On a compact set $K$, a continuous function is automatically uniformly continuous: the same $\delta$ works everywhere in $K$, given $\varepsilon$. On non-compact domains this fails — $f(x)=x^2$ is continuous on $\mathbb{R}$ but not uniformly continuous; $f(x)=1/x$ is continuous on $(0,1]$ but not uniformly continuous either.
See also
  • Compactness
Used in
Real analysis · Differentiation
Real analysis · Riemann integral
Real analysis · Uniform convergence

3. Differentiation

The derivative at $a$ is a number $f'(a)$, but the right way to read its definition is as a best-linear-approximation statement. A function $f$ is differentiable at $a$ if there exists a linear map $L\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ (multiplication by the single number $f'(a)$) such that

$$ f(a+h) = f(a) + L(h) + r(h),\quad \text{with}\quad \frac{|r(h)|}{|h|}\longrightarrow 0 \text{ as } h\to 0. $$

Equivalently $f'(a)=\lim_{h\to 0}\big(f(a+h)-f(a)\big)/h$. The packaging matters: in §6 the "linear map $L$" becomes the Jacobian, and the limit condition generalizes verbatim.

Best linear approximation & mean value theorem
drag $a$; compare tangent slope to secant slope
a = 1.20
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#mvt-svg'), out=$('#mvt-out');
  const sa=$('#mvt-a'), la=$('#mvt-aval'), fn=$('#mvt-fn');
  const W=640,H=340,pl=40,pr=20,pt=20,pb=40;
  const xmin=0, xmax=3, ymin=-1.6, ymax=1.6;
  function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
  const FNS = {
    sin:  { f:x=>Math.sin(x),          df:x=>Math.cos(x),              name:'sin x',  d:'cos x' },
    poly: { f:x=>x*x*x/6 - x,          df:x=>x*x/2 - 1,                name:'x³/6 − x', d:'x²/2 − 1' },
    exp:  { f:x=>Math.exp(x/2)-1,      df:x=>0.5*Math.exp(x/2),        name:'e^(x/2) − 1', d:'½e^(x/2)' }
  };
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const a=+sa.value; la.textContent=`a = ${a.toFixed(2)}`;
    const F=FNS[fn.value];
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:pt,x2:X(0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // curve
    const pts=[]; const N=260;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      pts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(F.f(x))}`);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    // endpoints A, B of [0, 3]
    const A=[0, F.f(0)], B=[3, F.f(3)];
    const secSlope=(B[1]-A[1])/(B[0]-A[0]);
    // secant AB
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(A[0]),y1:Y(A[1]),x2:X(B[0]),y2:Y(B[1]),
      stroke:'var(--violet)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'5 4'}));
    // tangent line at a
    const slope=F.df(a), fa=F.f(a);
    const tx1=xmin, ty1=fa+slope*(tx1-a);
    const tx2=xmax, ty2=fa+slope*(tx2-a);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(tx1),y1:Y(ty1),x2:X(tx2),y2:Y(ty2),
      stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':2}));
    // point (a, f(a))
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(a),cy:Y(fa),r:5,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.2}));
    // find MVT point c where f'(c)=secSlope
    let cFound=null;
    // scan
    let prev=F.df(xmin)-secSlope;
    for(let i=1;i<=400;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/400;
      const cur=F.df(x)-secSlope;
      if(prev*cur<=0){ cFound=x; break; }
      prev=cur;
    }
    if(cFound!==null){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(cFound),cy:Y(F.f(cFound)),r:5,fill:'var(--green)',stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.2}));
      const t=SVG('text',{x:X(cFound)+6,y:Y(F.f(cFound))-8,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--green)'});
      t.textContent='c: f′(c)=avg slope'; svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // endpoint markers
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(A[0]),cy:Y(A[1]),r:4,fill:'var(--violet)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(B[0]),cy:Y(B[1]),r:4,fill:'var(--violet)'}));
    // legend text
    const lg=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8, y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--yellow)'});
    lg.textContent='tangent at a'; svg.appendChild(lg);
    const lg2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8, y:pt+30,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--violet)'});
    lg2.textContent='secant from 0 to 3'; svg.appendChild(lg2);
    out.textContent =
      `f(x) = ${F.name}   ,   f′(x) = ${F.d}\n`+
      `tangent slope at a = ${a.toFixed(2)} :  f′(a) = ${slope.toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `mean-value slope  (f(3)-f(0))/3     =  ${secSlope.toFixed(4)}\n`+
      (cFound!==null
        ? `MVT guarantees some c ∈ (0,3) with f′(c) = avg slope. Found  c ≈ ${cFound.toFixed(3)}.`
        : `MVT: no c found in scan — try another function.`);
  }
  sa.addEventListener('input',render); fn.addEventListener('change',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
The chain rule as composition of best approximations

If $g$ is differentiable at $a$ and $f$ is differentiable at $g(a)$, the composition $f\circ g$ is differentiable at $a$ with $(f\circ g)'(a) = f'(g(a))\cdot g'(a)$. Written in best-linear-approximation language, the linear approximation to $f\circ g$ at $a$ is just the composition of the two linear approximations. The product of derivatives is the statement that "composition of one-dimensional linear maps is multiplication."

The mean value theorem

MVT. If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$, then there exists $c\in(a,b)$ with

$$ f'(c) = \frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}. $$

The widget above exhibits such a $c$ as the green dot. MVT is the workhorse of single-variable analysis: it underwrites Taylor's theorem, the sign of $f'$ controlling monotonicity, and L'Hôpital's rule. Its proof is a one-line reduction to Rolle's theorem, which is in turn an EVT argument.

Taylor's theorem with remainder

If $f$ is $n$-times differentiable on $[a,b]$ and $f^{(n+1)}$ exists on $(a,b)$, then for each $x\in[a,b]$ there exists $\xi$ between $a$ and $x$ such that

$$ f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{f^{(k)}(a)}{k!}(x-a)^k \;+\; \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x-a)^{n+1}. $$

The first sum is the degree-$n$ Taylor polynomial; the last term is Lagrange's form of the remainder (integral and Cauchy forms also exist). The remainder quantifies how well polynomials approximate smooth functions — tightly, if $f^{(n+1)}$ is bounded — and underlies every error estimate in numerical analysis.

Used in
Complex Analysis · Amplitwist
Dynamical systems · Orbits, flows, and vector fields
Real analysis · Bump functions & mollification
Real analysis · Fundamental theorem of calculus (both parts)
Real analysis · Multivariable differentiation

4. Uniform convergence

Given a sequence of functions $f_n\colon D\to\mathbb{R}$, two convergence notions compete:

  • Pointwise: $f_n\to f$ pointwise if $\forall x\in D\;\forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists N(x,\varepsilon)\;\forall n\ge N: |f_n(x)-f(x)|<\varepsilon$. The $N$ is allowed to depend on the point $x$.
  • Uniform: $f_n\to f$ uniformly if $\forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists N(\varepsilon)\;\forall n\ge N\;\forall x\in D: |f_n(x)-f(x)|<\varepsilon$. A single $N$ works for every $x$ simultaneously.

The quantifier swap is everything. Uniform convergence is equivalent to $\sup_{x\in D}|f_n(x)-f(x)|\to 0$, and it is the kind of convergence that preserves continuity, boundedness, and Riemann integrability.

Pointwise vs uniform convergence
x^n on [0,1] is pointwise but not uniform
n = 4

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#unif-svg'), out=$('#unif-out');
  const fam=$('#unif-fam'), sn=$('#unif-n'), nl=$('#unif-nval');
  const W=640, H=320, pl=45, pr=20, pt=20, pb=40;
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const n=+sn.value; nl.textContent=`n = ${n}`;
    let xmin=0, xmax=1, ymin=-0.2, ymax=1.1, fn, limitFn, name, isUniform, limitName;
    if(fam.value==='xn'){
      xmin=0; xmax=1; ymin=-0.1; ymax=1.1;
      fn = x => Math.pow(x,n);
      limitFn = x => (x<1? 0:1);
      name='xⁿ'; limitName='f(x)=0 on [0,1), f(1)=1 (discontinuous!)';
      isUniform=false;
    } else if(fam.value==='sinn'){
      xmin=0; xmax=Math.PI; ymin=-0.6; ymax=0.6;
      fn = x => Math.sin(n*x)/n;
      limitFn = x => 0;
      name='sin(nx)/n'; limitName='f(x) = 0';
      isUniform=true;
    } else {
      xmin=0; xmax=1; ymin=-0.2; ymax=1.6;
      fn = x => n*x*Math.pow(1-x,n);
      limitFn = x => 0;
      name='n x (1-x)ⁿ'; limitName='f(x) = 0 pointwise (but sup = (n/(n+1))^(n+1) ≈ 1/e does NOT go to 0)';
      isUniform=false;
    }
    function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
    function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(xmin),y1:pt,x2:X(xmin),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // limit function (green)
    const lpts=[]; const N=260;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      lpts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(limitFn(x))}`);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:lpts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'5 4'}));
    // f_n (pink)
    const fpts=[]; let sup=0;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      const y=fn(x);
      fpts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(y)}`);
      sup = Math.max(sup, Math.abs(y - limitFn(x)));
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:fpts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    // legend
    const l1=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
    l1.textContent=`f_n(x) = ${name}`; svg.appendChild(l1);
    const l2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+30,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--green)'});
    l2.textContent='pointwise limit f'; svg.appendChild(l2);
    out.textContent =
      `family : f_n(x) = ${name}\n`+
      `limit  : ${limitName}\n`+
      `current n = ${n}\n`+
      `sup |f_n − f| on domain ≈ ${sup.toFixed(4)}\n\n`+
      (isUniform
        ? `convergence is UNIFORM: sup → 0 as n → ∞.`
        : `convergence is POINTWISE but NOT UNIFORM: sup does not go to 0.`);
  }
  fam.addEventListener('change',render); sn.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Two clean theorems

Continuity preservation. If each $f_n$ is continuous on $D$ and $f_n\to f$ uniformly on $D$, then $f$ is continuous on $D$. The proof is a $3\varepsilon/3$ argument: write

$$ |f(x)-f(y)| \le |f(x)-f_N(x)| + |f_N(x)-f_N(y)| + |f_N(y)-f(y)|, $$

bound the outer terms by uniformity and the middle term by continuity of $f_N$. Pointwise convergence is not enough — the pointwise limit of $x^n$ on $[0,1]$ is discontinuous at $1$.

Integration swap. If each $f_n$ is Riemann-integrable on $[a,b]$ and $f_n\to f$ uniformly, then $f$ is Riemann-integrable and $\int_a^b f_n\to\int_a^b f$. Pointwise convergence on its own does not license this swap — compare the Dirichlet example from measure theory, §1.

Weierstrass M-test. If $\sum M_n<\infty$ and $|f_n(x)|\le M_n$ for all $x\in D$, then $\sum f_n$ converges absolutely and uniformly on $D$. This is the standard tool for producing uniform convergence of power series on compact subsets of their disk of convergence, and Fourier series of sufficiently smooth functions.

Used in
Complex Analysis · Normal families (Montel)
Functional analysis · Distributions and test functions
Measure theory · Monotone and dominated convergence
Real analysis · C[a,b] & the Arzelà–Ascoli theorem
Real analysis · Power series & radius of convergence

5. The Riemann integral

A partition of $[a,b]$ is a finite sequence $P\colon a=x_0upper and lower sums

$$ U(f,P)=\sum_{k=1}^n \Big(\sup_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} f\Big)(x_k-x_{k-1}),\qquad L(f,P)=\sum_{k=1}^n \Big(\inf_{[x_{k-1},x_k]} f\Big)(x_k-x_{k-1}). $$

Refining $P$ makes $U$ go down and $L$ go up. Define the upper and lower Darboux integrals as the $\inf_P U$ and $\sup_P L$. When these agree, $f$ is Riemann-integrable and their common value is $\int_a^b f$.

Riemann upper/lower sums
increase the partition size and watch the gap shrink
n = 8
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#riem-svg'), out=$('#riem-out');
  const fn=$('#riem-fn'), sn=$('#riem-n'), nl=$('#riem-nval');
  const W=640, H=320, pl=40, pr=20, pt=20, pb=40;
  const xmin=0, xmax=1, ymin=0, ymax=1.6;
  function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
  const FNS = {
    smooth: { f: x => (Math.sin(Math.PI*x)+1)/2 + x/4, name:'smooth',
              analytic: ( ( -Math.cos(Math.PI)+Math.cos(0) )/Math.PI/2 + 0.5 + 1/8 ),
              inner: (a,b,max)=>{ // actual sup/inf by sampling
                let lo=Infinity,hi=-Infinity;
                const S=16; for(let i=0;i<=S;i++){const x=a+(b-a)*i/S; const y=(Math.sin(Math.PI*x)+1)/2 + x/4; if(y<lo)lo=y; if(y>hi)hi=y;}
                return {lo,hi};
              } },
    step: { f: x => Math.floor(3*x)/3 + 0.1, name:'step',
            analytic: (0.1 + (1/3)*1/3 + (2/3)*1/3),
            inner: (a,b)=>{
              let lo=Infinity,hi=-Infinity;
              const S=20; for(let i=0;i<=S;i++){const x=a+(b-a)*i/S; const y=Math.floor(3*x)/3 + 0.1; if(y<lo)lo=y; if(y>hi)hi=y;}
              return {lo,hi};
            } },
    dirichlet: { f: x => { // plotted as 1 if x close to small-denom rational, else 0
                  for(let q=1;q<=8;q++){ const p=Math.round(q*x); if(Math.abs(x-p/q)<1e-4) return 1; }
                  return 0;
                },
                name:'dirichlet-like',
                analytic: NaN,
                inner: (a,b)=>({lo:0, hi:1}) }  // always 0 and 1 on any sub-interval (limit case)
  };
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const n=+sn.value; nl.textContent=`n = ${n}`;
    const F=FNS[fn.value];
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:pt,x2:X(0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // boxes: upper (pink translucent), lower (blue translucent)
    let U=0, L=0;
    for(let k=0;k<n;k++){
      const a=k/n, b=(k+1)/n;
      const {lo,hi}=F.inner(a,b);
      U += hi*(b-a); L += lo*(b-a);
      // upper box
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:X(a),y:Y(hi),width:X(b)-X(a),height:Y(0)-Y(hi),
        fill:'rgba(224,122,95,0.18)',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':0.8}));
      // lower box outline
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:X(a),y:Y(lo),width:X(b)-X(a),height:Y(0)-Y(lo),
        fill:'rgba(88,196,221,0.22)',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':0.8}));
    }
    // curve on top
    const pts=[]; const N=300;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      pts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(F.f(x))}`);
    }
    if(fn.value==='dirichlet'){
      // draw scattered 1s
      for(let q=1;q<=8;q++){ for(let p=0;p<=q;p++){ const xv=p/q; svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(xv),cy:Y(1),r:1.8,fill:'var(--yellow)'})); } }
      // and the "0" baseline in yellow
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:Y(0),x2:X(1),y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':2}));
    } else {
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':2}));
    }
    // legend
    const lg=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
    lg.textContent='U(f,P)  (upper sum)'; svg.appendChild(lg);
    const lg2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+30,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--blue)'});
    lg2.textContent='L(f,P)  (lower sum)'; svg.appendChild(lg2);
    const ans = Number.isFinite(F.analytic) ? F.analytic.toFixed(4) : '— (undefined: Riemann-non-integrable)';
    out.textContent =
      `f = ${F.name}   ,   partition size n = ${n}\n`+
      `U(f, P) = ${U.toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `L(f, P) = ${L.toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `gap U − L = ${(U-L).toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `∫₀¹ f  (analytic) = ${ans}\n`+
      (fn.value==='dirichlet'
        ? `\nFor the Dirichlet-like indicator, every subinterval contains both rationals and irrationals, so sup=1 and inf=0 on every piece — the gap U−L stays at 1 for every partition. Riemann-nonintegrable.`
        : fn.value==='step'
          ? `\nThe step function is bounded with finitely many discontinuities — Riemann-integrable. The gap shrinks like O(1/n).`
          : `\nContinuous f on [0,1] is Riemann-integrable; U and L squeeze together at rate O(1/n).`);
  }
  fn.addEventListener('change',render); sn.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
When is $f$ Riemann-integrable?

A bounded function is Riemann-integrable on $[a,b]$ iff for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a partition with $U(f,P)-L(f,P)<\varepsilon$. A clean characterization due to Lebesgue: $f$ is Riemann-integrable iff $f$ is bounded and its set of discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero. Every continuous function on $[a,b]$ qualifies; every monotone function qualifies; the Dirichlet indicator $\mathbf 1_{\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]}$ does not.

The fundamental theorem of calculus

FTC I. If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$, then $F$ is differentiable on $(a,b)$ with $F'(x)=f(x)$. So indefinite integration undoes differentiation.

FTC II. If $F$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and $F'=f$ on $(a,b)$ with $f$ Riemann-integrable, then $\int_a^b f=F(b)-F(a)$. So differentiation undoes indefinite integration.

Bridge to measure theory. Pointwise limits break Riemann integrability (Dirichlet); unbounded domains produce only conditional convergence (Fourier's integrals); the class of integrable functions is not a Banach space. These pathologies motivate Lebesgue's slice-the-range construction in measure theory, whose dominated convergence theorem licenses the swap $\int\lim = \lim\int$ under a single integrable dominator.

Used in
Dirichlet series and Euler products · Dirichlet series basics
Measure theory · σ-algebras and measures
Power sums, Bernoulli numbers, and Euler-Maclaurin · Euler-Maclaurin summation
Real analysis · Fundamental theorem of calculus (both parts)
Real analysis · Multivariable integration

6. Multivariable differentiation

A map $F\colon U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ is differentiable at $a\in U$ if there exists a linear map $L\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ such that

$$ F(a+h) = F(a) + L(h) + r(h),\qquad \frac{\|r(h)\|}{\|h\|}\longrightarrow 0 \text{ as } h\to 0. $$

The linear map $L$ is the total derivative $DF(a)\in\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{R}^n,\mathbb{R}^m)$. In the standard basis it is represented by the Jacobian matrix

$$ J_F(a) = \Big[\partial_j F^i(a)\Big]_{\substack{1\le i\le m\\ 1\le j\le n}}. $$

The $i$-th row is the gradient $\nabla F^i$ of the $i$-th component; the $j$-th column is the partial derivative vector $\partial_j F$.

Partials are not enough. Existence of all partial derivatives $\partial_j F^i(a)$ does not imply differentiability. The classical counterexample $f(x,y)=xy/(x^2+y^2)$ (with $f(0,0)=0$) has $\partial_x f(0,0)=\partial_y f(0,0)=0$, yet $f$ is not even continuous at the origin. Sufficient condition: if all partials exist on a neighborhood of $a$ and are continuous at $a$, then $F$ is (totally) differentiable at $a$ — i.e., $C^1$ implies $C^1$-differentiable in the strong sense.

Gradient and directional derivative

For scalar $f\colon U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$, the gradient $\nabla f(a)=(\partial_1 f,\dots,\partial_n f)^T$ is the vector whose dot product with a unit direction $v$ gives the directional derivative

$$ D_v f(a) = \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(a+tv)-f(a)}{t} = \nabla f(a)\cdot v. $$

Hence $\nabla f(a)$ points in the direction of steepest ascent and $\|\nabla f(a)\|$ is the steepest slope. Level sets $\{f=c\}$ are perpendicular to $\nabla f$ wherever $\nabla f\ne 0$.

Gradient field of $f(x,y)$
click the heatmap to place the probe
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#grad-svg'), out=$('#grad-out');
  const fn=$('#grad-fn');
  const W=640, H=360, pl=40, pr=20, pt=20, pb=40;
  const xmin=-2, xmax=2, ymin=-2, ymax=2;
  function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
  function iX(px){ return xmin + (px-pl)/(W-pl-pr)*(xmax-xmin); }
  function iY(py){ return ymin + ((H-pb)-py)/(H-pt-pb)*(ymax-ymin); }
  const FNS = {
    quad:   { f:(x,y)=>x*x + 2*y*y,            grad:(x,y)=>[2*x, 4*y],            name:'x² + 2y²',    d:'(2x, 4y)' },
    saddle: { f:(x,y)=>x*x - y*y,               grad:(x,y)=>[2*x, -2*y],           name:'x² − y²',    d:'(2x, −2y)' },
    trig:   { f:(x,y)=>Math.sin(x)*Math.cos(y), grad:(x,y)=>[Math.cos(x)*Math.cos(y), -Math.sin(x)*Math.sin(y)], name:'sin x · cos y', d:'(cos x cos y, −sin x sin y)' }
  };
  let px=1, py=0.6;
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const F=FNS[fn.value];
    // heatmap
    const cols=32, rows=24;
    let vmin=Infinity, vmax=-Infinity;
    for(let i=0;i<cols;i++) for(let j=0;j<rows;j++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*(i+.5)/cols;
      const y=ymin+(ymax-ymin)*(j+.5)/rows;
      const v=F.f(x,y); if(v<vmin)vmin=v; if(v>vmax)vmax=v;
    }
    const dw=(W-pl-pr)/cols, dh=(H-pt-pb)/rows;
    for(let i=0;i<cols;i++) for(let j=0;j<rows;j++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*(i+.5)/cols;
      const y=ymin+(ymax-ymin)*(j+.5)/rows;
      const v=F.f(x,y);
      const t=(v-vmin)/(vmax-vmin+1e-9);
      // purple(lo) → cyan(hi) ramp
      const r = Math.round(60 + 100*t), g = Math.round(40 + 180*t), b = Math.round(120 + 80*(1-t));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:pl+i*dw, y:pt+j*dh, width:dw+0.5, height:dh+0.5,
        fill:`rgb(${r},${g},${b})`, 'fill-opacity':0.75, stroke:'none'}));
    }
    // a few gradient arrows on a grid
    const gcols=10, grows=8;
    for(let i=0;i<gcols;i++) for(let j=0;j<grows;j++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*(i+.5)/gcols;
      const y=ymin+(ymax-ymin)*(j+.5)/grows;
      const [gx,gy]=F.grad(x,y);
      const len=Math.hypot(gx,gy);
      if(len<1e-6) continue;
      const s=0.2; // scale
      const x2=x+gx/len*s, y2=y+gy/len*s;
      drawArrow(svg, [X(x),Y(y)], [X(x2),Y(y2)],
        {color:'rgba(255,216,102,0.65)', markerId:'grad-a', width:1.2, pad1:2, pad2:6});
    }
    // probe point
    const [gx,gy]=F.grad(px,py);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(px),cy:Y(py),r:5,fill:'var(--pink)',stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.3}));
    const glen=Math.hypot(gx,gy);
    if(glen>1e-6){
      const s=0.5/glen*Math.min(1, glen);
      drawArrow(svg, [X(px),Y(py)], [X(px+gx*0.4),Y(py+gy*0.4)],
        {color:'var(--pink)', markerId:'probe-a', width:2.2});
    }
    // tangent plane readout
    const fa=F.f(px,py);
    out.textContent =
      `f(x,y) = ${F.name}     ,     ∇f = ${F.d}\n`+
      `probe  (x₀, y₀) = (${px.toFixed(2)}, ${py.toFixed(2)})\n`+
      `f(x₀, y₀) = ${fa.toFixed(3)}\n`+
      `∇f(x₀, y₀) = (${gx.toFixed(3)}, ${gy.toFixed(3)})     ‖∇f‖ = ${glen.toFixed(3)}\n`+
      `tangent plane :  z ≈ ${fa.toFixed(3)} + ${gx.toFixed(3)}·(x−${px.toFixed(2)}) + ${gy.toFixed(3)}·(y−${py.toFixed(2)})`;
  }
  svg.addEventListener('click', e=>{
    const rect=svg.getBoundingClientRect();
    const ax=((e.clientX-rect.left)/rect.width)*W;
    const ay=((e.clientY-rect.top)/rect.height)*H;
    const x=iX(ax), y=iY(ay);
    if(x>=xmin && x<=xmax && y>=ymin && y<=ymax){
      px=x; py=y; render();
    }
  });
  fn.addEventListener('change',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Chain rule

If $G\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$ is differentiable at $a$ and $F\colon\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}^k$ is differentiable at $G(a)$, then $F\circ G$ is differentiable at $a$ with

$$ D(F\circ G)(a) = DF(G(a))\circ DG(a),\qquad J_{F\circ G}(a) = J_F(G(a))\cdot J_G(a). $$

The derivative of a composition is the matrix product of Jacobians. In single-variable language this collapses to $(f\circ g)'(a)=f'(g(a))g'(a)$.

Used in
Real analysis · Inverse & implicit function theorems
Real analysis · Multivariable integration

7. Multivariable integration

Integration on $\mathbb{R}^n$ is built on boxes. A closed box is a product $B=[a_1,b_1]\times\cdots\times[a_n,b_n]$; its volume is $\operatorname{vol}(B)=\prod (b_j-a_j)$. A bounded function $f\colon B\to\mathbb{R}$ is Riemann-integrable if upper and lower sums over grid-partitions of $B$ agree, and when they do, $\int_B f$ is their common value. This generalizes §5 verbatim.

Fubini's theorem

Fubini (Riemann form). If $f$ is Riemann-integrable on $B=[a,b]\times[c,d]$ and for each $x\in[a,b]$ the single-variable function $y\mapsto f(x,y)$ is Riemann-integrable on $[c,d]$, then the iterated integral equals the double integral:

$$ \iint_{B} f \;=\; \int_a^b\!\!\bigg(\int_c^d f(x,y)\,dy\bigg)dx \;=\; \int_c^d\!\!\bigg(\int_a^b f(x,y)\,dx\bigg)dy. $$

Continuity of $f$ is the usual sufficient hypothesis; Tonelli's theorem extends this to nonnegative measurable functions in the measure-theoretic setting.

Change of variables

Let $\varphi\colon U\to V$ be a $C^1$-diffeomorphism between open sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $f\colon V\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous with compact support in $V$. Then

$$ \int_V f(y)\,dy = \int_U f(\varphi(x))\,|\det J_\varphi(x)|\,dx. $$

The factor $|\det J_\varphi|$ is how local volumes get rescaled by $\varphi$: a small box of volume $dV$ at $x$ is mapped by the linearization to a parallelepiped of volume $|\det J_\varphi(x)|\,dV$.

Area scaling by $|\det J_\varphi|$

<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#jac-svg'), out=$('#jac-out');
  const fn=$('#jac-fn'), su=$('#jac-u'), sv=$('#jac-v');
  const W=640, H=340, pad=30;
  // left panel: (u,v) domain box [0,2]x[0,1.5]; right panel: image
  const L={x:pad, y:pad, w:(W-3*pad)/2, h:H-2*pad, umin:0, umax:2, vmin:0, vmax:1.6};
  const R={x:pad*2+L.w, y:pad, w:(W-3*pad)/2, h:H-2*pad};
  const MAPS = {
    polar:  { f:(u,v)=>[u*Math.cos(v), u*Math.sin(v)], J:(u,v)=>u, xr:[-2,2], yr:[-2,2], name:'(u cos v, u sin v)', det:'u' },
    scale:  { f:(u,v)=>[2*u, 3*v],                       J:()=>6,     xr:[0,4], yr:[0,5], name:'(2u, 3v)', det:'6' },
    shear:  { f:(u,v)=>[u+v*v, v],                       J:(u,v)=>1,  xr:[0,4], yr:[0,2], name:'(u+v², v)', det:'1' }
  };
  function LX(u){ return L.x + (u-L.umin)/(L.umax-L.umin)*L.w; }
  function LY(v){ return L.y+L.h - (v-L.vmin)/(L.vmax-L.vmin)*L.h; }
  function RX(x, M){ return R.x + (x-M.xr[0])/(M.xr[1]-M.xr[0])*R.w; }
  function RY(y, M){ return R.y+R.h - (y-M.yr[0])/(M.yr[1]-M.yr[0])*R.h; }
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const u=+su.value, v=+sv.value;
    const M=MAPS[fn.value];
    // panel frames
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:L.x,y:L.y,width:L.w,height:L.h,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:R.x,y:R.y,width:R.w,height:R.h,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    // panel titles
    const t1=SVG('text',{x:L.x+L.w/2,y:L.y-6,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t1.textContent='domain (u, v)'; svg.appendChild(t1);
    const t2=SVG('text',{x:R.x+R.w/2,y:R.y-6,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t2.textContent='image φ(u, v)'; svg.appendChild(t2);
    // small rectangle [u,u+du]x[v,v+dv]
    const du=0.3, dv=0.25;
    // draw in domain
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:LX(u), y:LY(v+dv), width:LX(u+du)-LX(u), height:LY(v)-LY(v+dv),
      fill:'rgba(88,196,221,0.25)', stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6}));
    // polyline of image (sample boundary of the rectangle with many points)
    const bdy=[];
    const N=24;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){ const t=i/N; const uu=u+du*t, vv=v; bdy.push([uu,vv]); }
    for(let i=1;i<=N;i++){ const t=i/N; const uu=u+du, vv=v+dv*t; bdy.push([uu,vv]); }
    for(let i=1;i<=N;i++){ const t=i/N; const uu=u+du-du*t, vv=v+dv; bdy.push([uu,vv]); }
    for(let i=1;i<=N;i++){ const t=i/N; const uu=u, vv=v+dv-dv*t; bdy.push([uu,vv]); }
    const imgPts = bdy.map(([uu,vv])=>M.f(uu,vv));
    // image polygon
    const ptsStr = imgPts.map(([x,y])=>`${RX(x,M)},${RY(y,M)}`).join(' ');
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polygon',{points:ptsStr, fill:'rgba(224,122,95,0.25)', stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.6}));
    // area by shoelace
    let area2=0;
    for(let i=0;i<imgPts.length;i++){
      const [x1,y1]=imgPts[i], [x2,y2]=imgPts[(i+1)%imgPts.length];
      area2 += (x1*y2 - x2*y1);
    }
    const imgArea=Math.abs(area2)/2;
    const domArea=du*dv;
    const det=M.J(u+du/2, v+dv/2);
    out.textContent =
      `φ : ${M.name}    ,    det J_φ = ${M.det}\n`+
      `rectangle at (u₀,v₀) = (${u.toFixed(2)}, ${v.toFixed(2)})  of  Δu·Δv = ${domArea.toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `|det J_φ(u₀+½Δu, v₀+½Δv)| = ${Math.abs(det).toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `predicted image area    = |det J_φ| · Δu·Δv ≈ ${(Math.abs(det)*domArea).toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `actual image area (shoelace) = ${imgArea.toFixed(4)}\n\n`+
      `as Δu, Δv → 0 the ratio (actual / predicted) → 1 — this is change-of-variables.`;
  }
  fn.addEventListener('change',render);
  su.addEventListener('input',render); sv.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
8. Inverse and implicit function theorems

Both theorems upgrade a statement about a linear map (the Jacobian) at a point to a statement about the nonlinear map near that point. They are the engine behind local coordinates on submanifolds — the starting point of differential geometry.

Inverse function theorem

IFT. Let $F\colon U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ be $C^1$ on an open set $U$ and suppose the Jacobian $J_F(a)$ is invertible at some $a\in U$. Then there exist open neighborhoods $V\ni a$ and $W\ni F(a)$ such that $F\colon V\to W$ is a $C^1$-diffeomorphism, and its inverse satisfies

$$ J_{F^{-1}}(F(a)) = J_F(a)^{-1}. $$

The proof idea: the linear approximation $DF(a)$ is invertible, and the nonlinear remainder is small by differentiability. A fixed-point (contraction-mapping) argument on the map $y\mapsto x + DF(a)^{-1}(y-F(x))$ produces the local inverse; the Banach fixed-point theorem does the analytic heavy lifting.

Implicit function theorem

Implicit FT. Let $F\colon U\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\times\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}^m$ be $C^1$, suppose $F(a,b)=0$, and assume the $m\times m$ Jacobian block $\partial F/\partial y(a,b)$ is invertible. Then there exist neighborhoods $V\ni a$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $W\ni b$ in $\mathbb{R}^m$ and a unique $C^1$ map $g\colon V\to W$ such that for all $x\in V$

$$ F(x, g(x)) = 0, \qquad g(a)=b. $$

Moreover, implicit differentiation gives $Dg(x) = -\big(\partial F/\partial y\big)^{-1}\cdot\big(\partial F/\partial x\big)$. The implicit FT is a restatement of the inverse FT applied to the map $(x,y)\mapsto (x,F(x,y))$.

Solving $F(x,y)=0$ locally
drag $x_0$; the widget solves for $y=g(x_0)$ when possible
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#ift-svg'), out=$('#ift-out');
  const fn=$('#ift-fn'), sx=$('#ift-x'), sb=$('#ift-branch');
  const W=640, H=340, pl=40, pr=20, pt=20, pb=40;
  const xmin=-1.8, xmax=1.8, ymin=-1.8, ymax=1.8;
  function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
  function solve(F, Fy, x0, yGuess){
    // Newton on y: F(x0, y) = 0
    let y=yGuess;
    for(let i=0;i<60;i++){
      const f=F(x0,y), dy=Fy(x0,y);
      if(Math.abs(dy)<1e-10) return null;
      const step=f/dy;
      y -= step;
      if(Math.abs(step)<1e-10) return y;
    }
    return null;
  }
  const FNS = {
    circle: { F:(x,y)=>x*x+y*y-1, Fx:(x,y)=>2*x, Fy:(x,y)=>2*y,
              name:'x² + y² − 1', pts:()=>{
                const L=[];
                for(let t=0;t<=2*Math.PI+0.01;t+=0.02) L.push([Math.cos(t), Math.sin(t)]);
                return L;
              }, upper:x=>Math.sqrt(Math.max(0,1-x*x)), lower:x=>-Math.sqrt(Math.max(0,1-x*x)) },
    cubic: { F:(x,y)=>y*y*y+y-x, Fx:(x,y)=>-1, Fy:(x,y)=>3*y*y+1,
              name:'y³ + y − x', pts:()=>{
                const L=[];
                for(let yy=-1.6;yy<=1.6;yy+=0.02){ const xx=yy*yy*yy+yy; if(xx>=xmin && xx<=xmax) L.push([xx,yy]); }
                return L;
              }, upper:x=>solve((a,b)=>b*b*b+b-a, (a,b)=>3*b*b+1, x, 1), lower:x=>solve((a,b)=>b*b*b+b-a, (a,b)=>3*b*b+1, x, -1) },
    folium: { F:(x,y)=>x*x*x+y*y*y-3*x*y, Fx:(x,y)=>3*x*x-3*y, Fy:(x,y)=>3*y*y-3*x,
              name:'x³ + y³ − 3xy', pts:()=>{
                const L=[];
                for(let t=-6;t<=6;t+=0.01){
                  const den=1+t*t*t;
                  if(Math.abs(den)<1e-3) continue;
                  const xx=3*t/den, yy=3*t*t/den;
                  if(xx>=xmin && xx<=xmax && yy>=ymin && yy<=ymax) L.push([xx,yy]);
                }
                return L;
              }, upper:x=>solve((a,b)=>a*a*a+b*b*b-3*a*b, (a,b)=>3*b*b-3*a, x, 1.2),
                  lower:x=>solve((a,b)=>a*a*a+b*b*b-3*a*b, (a,b)=>3*b*b-3*a, x, -1.2) }
  };
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const x0=+sx.value;
    const F=FNS[fn.value];
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:pt,x2:X(0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // zero-set
    const curve=F.pts();
    if(curve.length){
      const ptStr=curve.map(([x,y])=>`${X(x)},${Y(y)}`).join(' ');
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:ptStr,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.8}));
    }
    // vertical line at x0
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(x0),y1:pt,x2:X(x0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4','stroke-width':1}));
    // solve
    let y0 = sb.value==='upper' ? F.upper(x0) : F.lower(x0);
    let msg;
    if(y0===null || !Number.isFinite(y0) || (fn.value==='circle' && Math.abs(x0)>1)){
      msg = `cannot solve F(x₀, y) = 0 near this branch/point.`;
    } else {
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(x0),cy:Y(y0),r:5,fill:'var(--yellow)',stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.2}));
      // tangent slope via implicit diff: dy/dx = -Fx/Fy
      const fx=F.Fx(x0,y0), fy=F.Fy(x0,y0);
      if(Math.abs(fy)<1e-8){
        msg = `F(x₀, y₀) = 0 at (${x0.toFixed(2)}, ${y0.toFixed(2)}), but ∂F/∂y ≈ 0 — implicit FT fails, curve is vertical here.`;
      } else {
        const slope=-fx/fy;
        // draw a short tangent segment
        const dx=0.4;
        svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{
          x1:X(x0-dx), y1:Y(y0-slope*dx), x2:X(x0+dx), y2:Y(y0+slope*dx),
          stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':2}));
        msg = `F(x₀,y₀) = 0 at  (${x0.toFixed(2)}, ${y0.toFixed(2)})\n`+
              `∂F/∂x = ${fx.toFixed(3)}   ,   ∂F/∂y = ${fy.toFixed(3)}\n`+
              `implicit diff :  dy/dx = −(∂F/∂x)/(∂F/∂y) = ${slope.toFixed(3)}\n`+
              `IFT applies : locally, y = g(x) with g′(x₀) = ${slope.toFixed(3)}.`;
      }
    }
    out.textContent = `F(x,y) = ${F.name}\nbranch = ${sb.value}\n\n${msg}`;
  }
  fn.addEventListener('change',render); sx.addEventListener('input',render); sb.addEventListener('change',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Why this is the gateway to manifolds

A subset $M\subseteq\mathbb{R}^N$ is a smooth $k$-dimensional submanifold if near each point it looks, after a $C^\infty$ change of coordinates, like $\mathbb{R}^k\subseteq\mathbb{R}^N$. The implicit function theorem is the mechanism: given a $C^1$ map $F\colon\mathbb{R}^N\to\mathbb{R}^{N-k}$ whose Jacobian has full rank at each point of $F^{-1}(0)$, the level set $M=F^{-1}(0)$ is automatically a $k$-dimensional submanifold, parametrized locally by $k$ of the coordinates. This regular-value theorem is the workhorse constructor of manifolds — see smooth manifolds for the coordinate-invariant formulation and the rank theorem, its cleanest generalization.

Where IFT fails. On the folium $x^3+y^3-3xy=0$, both partials vanish at the origin — so near $(0,0)$ the curve is not the graph of $y=g(x)$ or of $x=h(y)$. Geometrically, it self-intersects. IFT guarantees nothing when the relevant Jacobian block is singular; the failure modes are where interesting singularity theory starts.

Used in
Smooth manifolds · Smooth manifolds and atlases

9. Numeric series & convergence tests

A numeric series is a formal sum $\sum_{n\ge 1} a_n$ of real terms; its partial sums $S_N=\sum_{n=1}^N a_n$ are the object that either converges or diverges. The series converges absolutely if $\sum |a_n|<\infty$ and conditionally if it converges but $\sum|a_n|=\infty$. Absolute convergence is invariant under reordering; conditional convergence is fragile — Riemann's rearrangement theorem says a conditionally convergent series can be rearranged to sum to any real number (or to $\pm\infty$).

The standard convergence tests

  • Comparison: $0\le a_n \le b_n$ and $\sum b_n<\infty$ imply $\sum a_n<\infty$.
  • Ratio test: if $L=\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|$ exists, then $L<1\Rightarrow$ abs. conv., $L>1\Rightarrow$ div., $L=1$ inconclusive.
  • Root test: if $L=\limsup |a_n|^{1/n}$, then $L<1\Rightarrow$ abs. conv., $L>1\Rightarrow$ div., $L=1$ inconclusive. Strictly stronger than ratio.
  • Integral test: for $f$ positive, decreasing, $\int_1^\infty f$ and $\sum f(n)$ converge together.
  • Abel / Dirichlet: for $\sum a_n b_n$, if $a_n\downarrow 0$ and the partial sums $B_N=\sum b_n$ are bounded, the series converges (generalizes the alternating series test).

Ratio-test explorer
plot $|a_{n+1}/a_n|$; verdict on convergence
p = 2.0 r = 0.50
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#ratio-svg'), out=$('#ratio-out');
  const fam=$('#ratio-fam'), sp=$('#ratio-p'), sr=$('#ratio-r');
  const pL=$('#ratio-pval'), rL=$('#ratio-rval');
  const W=640, H=320, pl=50, pr=20, pt=30, pb=40;
  const NMAX=40;
  function X(n){ return pl + (n-1)/(NMAX-1)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function Y(y, ymax){ return H-pb - (y/ymax)*(H-pt-pb); }
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const p=+sp.value, r=+sr.value;
    pL.textContent=`p = ${p.toFixed(1)}`;
    rL.textContent=`r = ${r.toFixed(2)}`;
    let term, limitL, name;
    switch(fam.value){
      case 'inv-p':
        term = n => 1/Math.pow(n,p);
        limitL = 1;
        name = `a_n = 1/n^${p.toFixed(1)}`; break;
      case 'inv-fact':
        term = n => { let v=1; for(let k=1;k<=n;k++) v/=k; return v; };
        limitL = 0;
        name = `a_n = 1/n!`; break;
      case 'factpow':
        term = n => { let v=1; for(let k=1;k<=n;k++) v=v*k/n; return v; };
        limitL = 1/Math.E;
        name = `a_n = n!/n^n`; break;
      case 'altharm':
        term = n => Math.pow(-1,n)/n;
        limitL = 1;
        name = `a_n = (-1)^n/n`; break;
      case 'nn-fact':
        term = n => { let v=1; for(let k=1;k<=n;k++) v=v*n/k; return v; };
        limitL = Math.E;
        name = `a_n = n^n/n!`; break;
      case 'geom':
        term = n => Math.pow(r,n)/Math.pow(n,p);
        limitL = r;
        name = `a_n = r^n / n^${p.toFixed(1)}`; break;
    }
    const ratios=[];
    for(let n=1;n<=NMAX;n++){
      const a = term(n), b = term(n+1);
      if(!isFinite(a) || !isFinite(b) || a===0){ ratios.push(NaN); continue; }
      ratios.push(Math.abs(b/a));
    }
    const ymax = Math.max(1.4, Math.min(4, Math.max(...ratios.filter(isFinite), limitL)*1.2));
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0,ymax),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0,ymax),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:pt,x2:pl,y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // y=1 reference
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(1,ymax),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(1,ymax),
      stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1,'stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
    const t1=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-4,y:Y(1,ymax)-4,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    t1.textContent='1'; svg.appendChild(t1);
    // limit L (green dashed)
    if(isFinite(limitL) && limitL>=0 && limitL<=ymax){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(limitL,ymax),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(limitL,ymax),
        stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'5 4'}));
      const t2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-4,y:Y(limitL,ymax)-4,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':11,fill:'var(--green)'});
      t2.textContent=`L = ${limitL.toFixed(3)}`; svg.appendChild(t2);
    }
    // ratios (pink polyline + dots)
    const pts=[];
    for(let i=0;i<ratios.length;i++){
      const v=ratios[i];
      if(!isFinite(v)) continue;
      const vc=Math.min(v,ymax);
      pts.push(`${X(i+1)},${Y(vc,ymax)}`);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(i+1),cy:Y(vc,ymax),r:2.5,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.8}));
    // x axis ticks
    for(const n of [1,10,20,30,40]){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(n),y1:H-pb,x2:X(n),y2:H-pb+5,stroke:'var(--mute)'}));
      const t=SVG('text',{x:X(n),y:H-pb+18,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent=String(n); svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // title
    const hd=SVG('text',{x:pl+4,y:pt-10,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
    hd.textContent = `|a_{n+1}/a_n|   —   ${name}`; svg.appendChild(hd);
    // verdict
    let verdict;
    if(limitL < 1 - 1e-8) verdict = `L = ${limitL.toFixed(3)} < 1  ⇒  series converges absolutely`;
    else if(limitL > 1 + 1e-8) verdict = `L = ${limitL.toFixed(3)} > 1  ⇒  series diverges (term does not go to 0)`;
    else verdict = `L = 1  ⇒  ratio test inconclusive — try root test, integral test, or the alternating-series test`;
    // root-test comparison
    let rootL = null;
    try {
      const aN = term(20);
      if(aN>0 && isFinite(aN)) rootL = Math.pow(aN, 1/20);
    } catch(e){}
    out.textContent =
      `series         :  ${name}\n`+
      `limit of ratio :  L = ${limitL.toFixed(6)}\n`+
      (rootL!==null ? `root test (est) :  |a_n|^{1/n} at n=20  ≈  ${rootL.toFixed(4)}\n` : '')+
      `\nverdict        :  ${verdict}`;
  }
  fam.addEventListener('change',render);
  sp.addEventListener('input',render);
  sr.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Rearrangement is fragile. For the alternating harmonic series $\sum (-1)^{n+1}/n = \ln 2$, one can rearrange the terms to sum to any real number $s$: alternately take positive terms until the partial sum exceeds $s$, then negative terms until it drops below, and so on. Both the positive and negative subseries diverge, so there is always more ammunition on each side. Absolute convergence prevents this chicanery because the positive and negative parts both converge.

Used in
Real analysis · Power series & radius of convergence

10. Power series & radius of convergence

A power series centered at $a$ is a formal sum $\sum_{n\ge 0} c_n (x-a)^n$. The Cauchy–Hadamard formula identifies a single number $R\in[0,\infty]$ — the radius of convergence — via

$$ \frac{1}{R} = \limsup_{n\to\infty} |c_n|^{1/n}. $$

The series converges absolutely for $|x-a|R$, and boundary behavior at $|x-a|=R$ depends sensitively on the coefficients. A power series converges uniformly on every compact subset of the open disk $(a-R, a+R)$ (by the Weierstrass M-test), which is why term-by-term differentiation and integration are legal inside the radius.

Power-series partial sums
compare $S_N(x)$ (pink) to the true function (green dashed)
N = 4
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#pseries-svg'), out=$('#pseries-out');
  const fn=$('#pseries-fn'), sN=$('#pseries-N'), nL=$('#pseries-Nval');
  const W=640,H=320,pl=45,pr=20,pt=20,pb=40;
  const FNS = {
    exp:   { true:x=>Math.exp(x), coef:n=>{ let v=1; for(let k=1;k<=n;k++) v/=k; return v; }, R:Infinity, xmin:-3, xmax:3, ymin:-0.5, ymax:8, name:'e^x' },
    sin:   { true:x=>Math.sin(x), coef:n=>{ if(n%2===0) return 0; const m=(n-1)/2; let v=1; for(let k=1;k<=n;k++) v/=k; return (m%2===0?1:-1)*v; }, R:Infinity, xmin:-6, xmax:6, ymin:-1.5, ymax:1.5, name:'sin x' },
    cos:   { true:x=>Math.cos(x), coef:n=>{ if(n%2!==0) return 0; const m=n/2; let v=1; for(let k=1;k<=n;k++) v/=k; return (m%2===0?1:-1)*v; }, R:Infinity, xmin:-6, xmax:6, ymin:-1.5, ymax:1.5, name:'cos x' },
    geom:  { true:x=>1/(1-x), coef:n=>1, R:1, xmin:-1.6, xmax:0.95, ymin:-3, ymax:8, name:'1/(1-x)' },
    log:   { true:x=>Math.log(1+x), coef:n=>{ if(n===0) return 0; return (n%2===1?1:-1)/n; }, R:1, xmin:-0.95, xmax:2, ymin:-3, ymax:1.5, name:'ln(1+x)' }
  };
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const N=+sN.value; nL.textContent=`N = ${N}`;
    const F=FNS[fn.value];
    const {xmin,xmax,ymin,ymax}=F;
    function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
    function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    if(xmin<=0 && xmax>=0){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:pt,x2:X(0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    }
    // convergence band (cyan translucent)
    if(isFinite(F.R)){
      const ax = Math.max(xmin, -F.R), bx = Math.min(xmax, F.R);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:X(ax),y:pt,width:X(bx)-X(ax),height:H-pt-pb,
        fill:'rgba(125,224,214,0.08)', stroke:'var(--cyan)','stroke-dasharray':'3 4','stroke-width':1}));
      const tt=SVG('text',{x:(X(ax)+X(bx))/2,y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
      tt.textContent = `|x| < R = ${F.R}`; svg.appendChild(tt);
    } else {
      const tt=SVG('text',{x:(pl+W-pr)/2,y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
      tt.textContent = `R = ∞  (converges everywhere)`; svg.appendChild(tt);
    }
    // true function (green dashed)
    const tpts=[]; const M=260;
    for(let i=0;i<=M;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/M;
      const y=F.true(x);
      if(isFinite(y) && y>=ymin-1 && y<=ymax+1) tpts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(y)}`);
      else tpts.push('');
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:tpts.filter(s=>s).join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'5 4'}));
    // partial sum S_N(x)
    function partialSum(x){ let s=0; for(let k=0;k<=N;k++){ s += F.coef(k)*Math.pow(x,k); } return s; }
    const ppts=[];
    for(let i=0;i<=M;i++){
      const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/M;
      const y=partialSum(x);
      if(isFinite(y) && y>=ymin-1 && y<=ymax+1) ppts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(y)}`);
      else ppts.push('');
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:ppts.filter(s=>s).join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    // legend
    const lg1=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+32,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
    lg1.textContent=`S_N(x)`; svg.appendChild(lg1);
    const lg2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+48,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--green)'});
    lg2.textContent=F.name; svg.appendChild(lg2);
    // sample error
    const x0 = (F.R===Infinity) ? 1 : 0.5;
    const e = Math.abs(partialSum(x0) - F.true(x0));
    out.textContent =
      `series : ${F.name}\n`+
      `R = ${F.R}\n`+
      `at x = ${x0}:   S_${N}(x) = ${partialSum(x0).toFixed(6)}   ,   f(x) = ${F.true(x0).toFixed(6)}\n`+
      `error  |S_N − f| = ${e.toExponential(3)}`;
  }
  fn.addEventListener('change',render); sN.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Bridge to complex analysis. A real-analytic function on an open interval is — via Cauchy–Hadamard — the restriction of a holomorphic function on an open disk in $\mathbb{C}$. Many identities, e.g. $\exp(x+y)=\exp(x)\exp(y)$, are easiest to prove once one has passed to the complex domain and invoked the identity theorem. See complex analysis for the holomorphic perspective on power series.

Differentiating inside the disk

If $f(x)=\sum c_n (x-a)^n$ has radius of convergence $R$, then $f$ is $C^\infty$ on $(a-R, a+R)$ and its derivative is given by term-by-term differentiation: $f'(x)=\sum n c_n (x-a)^{n-1}$, with the same $R$. This is an immediate consequence of uniform convergence on compact sub-intervals: swap sum and $d/dx$. Boundary values are a separate matter — Abel's theorem handles the case when the series converges at an endpoint.

11. Metric completeness & Baire category

A metric space $(X,d)$ is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges in $X$. Completeness is not a topological property — the open interval $(0,1)$ is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}$ (which is complete), yet $(0,1)$ with the inherited metric is not complete: $1/n\to 0\notin(0,1)$. Every metric space has a unique (up to isometry) completion $\widehat X$: a complete metric space containing $X$ as a dense subspace. The standard construction mimics Cantor's completion of $\mathbb{Q}$: take equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences.

Baire's theorem

A subset $A\subseteq X$ is nowhere dense if its closure has empty interior: $\mathrm{int}(\overline A)=\varnothing$. A countable union of nowhere-dense sets is called meager (or of "first category"). Baire's theorem says: a complete metric space is not meager in itself. Equivalently, a countable intersection of open dense subsets is dense.

This is a staple of functional analysis: the uniform boundedness principle, open mapping theorem, and closed graph theorem all reduce to Baire. The proof is a nested-balls construction — at each step, pick a smaller closed ball avoiding the next nowhere-dense piece; completeness delivers a point in the intersection.

Baire demo: rationals vs. complete spaces
countable unions of nowhere-dense singletons
k = 8
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#baire-svg'), out=$('#baire-out');
  const sk=$('#baire-k'), kL=$('#baire-kval'), vw=$('#baire-view');
  const W=640, H=320, pl=40, pr=20, pt=40, pb=40;
  const xmin=0, xmax=1;
  function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const k=+sk.value; kL.textContent=`k = ${k}`;
    // main axis
    const yAx = H/2;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:yAx,x2:W-pr,y2:yAx,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1.4}));
    // endpoint ticks
    for(const t of [0,0.25,0.5,0.75,1]){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(t),y1:yAx-5,x2:X(t),y2:yAx+5,stroke:'var(--mute)'}));
      const lab=SVG('text',{x:X(t),y:yAx+20,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lab.textContent=t.toFixed(2); svg.appendChild(lab);
    }
    // enumerate rationals with denom<=k
    const rats=new Set();
    for(let q=1;q<=k;q++){ for(let p=0;p<=q;p++){ rats.add(p/q); } }
    const arr=[...rats].sort((a,b)=>a-b);
    if(vw.value==='rationals'){
      // plot each as a singleton
      for(const x of arr){
        svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:X(x),cy:yAx,r:2.5,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
      }
      // labels
      const t1=SVG('text',{x:pl,y:pt-10,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
      t1.textContent = `${arr.length} rationals plotted — each is nowhere dense (a singleton)`;
      svg.appendChild(t1);
      const t2=SVG('text',{x:pl,y:pt+8,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t2.textContent = `as k→∞, you enumerate ℚ∩[0,1]: a countable union of nowhere-dense sets`;
      svg.appendChild(t2);
    } else {
      // countable open-dense: show U_n = [0,1] minus k-th rational's ε-ball
      // paint multiple thin "holes" at each rational to illustrate shrinking covers
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:X(0),y:yAx-12,width:X(1)-X(0),height:24,fill:'rgba(131,193,103,0.14)',stroke:'var(--green)'}));
      // carve out small neighborhoods
      for(let i=0;i<arr.length;i++){
        const q = arr[i];
        const eps = 0.5 / (i+2) / k;  // shrinking holes
        const ax = Math.max(0, q-eps), bx = Math.min(1, q+eps);
        svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:X(ax),y:yAx-12,width:X(bx)-X(ax),height:24,fill:'var(--bg)'}));
      }
      const t1=SVG('text',{x:pl,y:pt-10,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--green)'});
      t1.textContent = `countable intersection ⋂ U_n  of open dense sets — still dense (Baire)`;
      svg.appendChild(t1);
      const t2=SVG('text',{x:pl,y:pt+8,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t2.textContent = `each U_n = [0,1] minus a shrinking ball around one rational`;
      svg.appendChild(t2);
    }
    out.textContent =
      `[0,1] is a complete metric space (closed bounded interval in R)\n`+
      `rationals enumerated so far : ${arr.length}\n`+
      (vw.value==='rationals'
        ? `singleton {q_k} is closed with empty interior ⇒ nowhere dense.\nUnion over all k is meager but DENSE.  Meager ≠ small in the topological sense.`
        : `complement of a nowhere-dense closed set is open and dense.\nBy Baire, countable intersection of open-dense sets is still dense in [0,1].\nCompare: ℝ ∖ ℚ (the irrationals) is a dense G_δ — hence uncountable.`);
  }
  sk.addEventListener('input',render);
  vw.addEventListener('change',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Baire fails on $\mathbb{Q}$. $\mathbb{Q}$ (with the subspace metric) is a countable union of singletons — each nowhere dense in $\mathbb{Q}$ (between any two rationals there is another). If Baire applied, $\mathbb{Q}$ would be non-meager in itself, contradiction. The resolution: $\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete — Cauchy sequences of rationals can limit to irrationals, escaping $\mathbb{Q}$. Completeness is an indispensable hypothesis.
See also
  • Metric spaces

12. Fundamental theorem of calculus (both parts)

The fundamental theorem of calculus has two halves that together exhibit integration and differentiation as inverse operations — modulo boundary terms.

  • Part I (integration → differentiation). If $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ is differentiable on $(a,b)$ with $F'(x) = f(x)$.
  • Part II (evaluation). If $F$ is differentiable on $[a,b]$ with $F' = f$ integrable, then $\int_a^b f = F(b) - F(a)$.

Part I is a one-line application of the mean value theorem for integrals. Part II requires a little more care — it holds when $F$ is differentiable and $F'$ is Riemann-integrable, but in the Lebesgue setting the correct class is absolutely continuous functions (the Cantor "devil's staircase" $c\colon[0,1]\to[0,1]$ is continuous and monotone with $c'=0$ a.e., yet $c(1)-c(0)=1\ne 0 = \int_0^1 c'$).

    const F = FNS[fn.value];
    const x = +sx.value; xL.textContent = `x = ${x.toFixed(2)}`;
    const xmin=0, xmax=3;
    const ymin = Math.min(0, -F.ymax*0.05);

    // left panel

    const rightW = W - midX - pr - 4;
    function XL(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin) * leftW; }
    function YL(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin) * (H-pt-pb); }
    function XR(v){ return midX + 4 + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin) * rightW; }
    function YR(v){
      const FM = F.F(xmax), Fm = Math.min(0, F.F(xmin)) - Math.abs(F.F(xmax))*0.05;
      return H-pb - (v-Fm)/(FM-Fm) * (H-pt-pb);
    }
    // left axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl, y1:YL(0), x2:pl+leftW, y2:YL(0), stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl, y1:pt, x2:pl, y2:H-pb, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // shade area under f from 0 to x (pink translucent)
    const N=140;
    const pts = [[XL(0),YL(0)]];
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const t = 0 + x*i/N;
      pts.push([XL(t), YL(F.f(t))]);
    }
    pts.push([XL(x),YL(0)]);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polygon',{
      points: pts.map(([a,b])=>`${a},${b}`).join(' '),
      fill:'rgba(224,122,95,0.22)', stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.2 }));
    // full curve f(t) pink
    const fullPts=[];
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const t = xmin + (xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      fullPts.push(`${XL(t)},${YL(F.f(t))}`);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:fullPts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
    // labels left
    const lt=SVG('text',{x:pl+8, y:pt+14, 'font-size':13, fill:'var(--pink)','font-weight':600});
    lt.textContent = `f(t) = ${F.name}`; svg.appendChild(lt);
    const lt2=SVG('text',{x:pl+8, y:pt+30, 'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    lt2.textContent = `shaded area = ∫₀ˣ f(t) dt`; svg.appendChild(lt2);
    // vertical line at x
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:XL(x),y1:pt,x2:XL(x),y2:H-pb, stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4'}));
    // x-axis tick at x
    const tx=SVG('text',{x:XL(x),y:H-pb+18,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11,fill:'var(--yellow)'});
    tx.textContent=`x = ${x.toFixed(2)}`; svg.appendChild(tx);
    // right axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:midX+4, y1:YR(0), x2:W-pr, y2:YR(0), stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:midX+4, y1:pt, x2:midX+4, y2:H-pb, stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    // F curve green
    const Fpts=[];
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const t = xmin + (xmax-xmin)*i/N;
      Fpts.push(`${XR(t)},${YR(F.F(t))}`);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:Fpts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2}));
    // moving dot
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:XR(x),cy:YR(F.F(x)),r:5,fill:'var(--green)',stroke:'#0b0f16','stroke-width':1.2}));
    const lr=SVG('text',{x:midX+16, y:pt+14,'font-size':13,fill:'var(--green)','font-weight':600});
    lr.textContent = `F(x) = ${F.Fname}`; svg.appendChild(lr);
    const lr2=SVG('text',{x:midX+16, y:pt+30,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    lr2.textContent = `F(x) grows at rate f(x)`; svg.appendChild(lr2);
    // numeric check: F'(x) vs f(x)
    const h=0.001;
    const numDeriv = (F.F(x+h)-F.F(x-h))/(2*h);
    out.textContent =
      `f(t) = ${F.name}                  ,   F(x) = ${F.Fname}\n`+
      `x          =  ${x.toFixed(4)}\n`+
      `∫₀ˣ f dt   =  F(x)  =  ${F.F(x).toFixed(6)}    (shaded area)\n`+
      `f(x)       =  ${F.f(x).toFixed(6)}\n`+
      `F'(x) (num)=  ${numDeriv.toFixed(6)}           ⇒  Part I:  F'(x) = f(x) ✓`;
  }
  fn.addEventListener('change',render); sx.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The Cantor function counterexample. Let $c\colon[0,1]\to[0,1]$ be the Cantor staircase: continuous, nondecreasing, $c(0)=0$, $c(1)=1$, and $c'(x)=0$ for every $x$ in the complement of the Cantor set (which has full measure). Then $\int_0^1 c'\,dx = 0$ in the Lebesgue sense, yet $c(1)-c(0)=1$. FTC Part II fails because $c$ is not absolutely continuous — it moves "by jumps on a null set," which AC forbids.

13. Bump functions & mollification

A bump function is a $C^\infty$ function with compact support. The prototype is

$$ \psi(x) = \begin{cases}\exp\!\big(-1/(1-x^2)\big), & |x| < 1,\\ 0, & |x|\ge 1.\end{cases} $$

$\psi$ is smooth on all of $\mathbb{R}$: the transition at $|x|=1$ is flat (every derivative of $\exp(-1/(1-x^2))$ vanishes as $|x|\to 1^-$), so the extension by zero yields a $C^\infty$ function. No polynomial can be compactly supported — a nonzero polynomial vanishing on an open interval is forced to be identically zero by the identity theorem. Bumps are the building block of smooth cutoffs and partitions of unity.

Mollification

Given $f\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R})$ and a smooth nonnegative bump $\eta$ with $\int\eta = 1$, let $\eta_\varepsilon(x) = \varepsilon^{-1}\eta(x/\varepsilon)$. The mollification

$$ f_\varepsilon(x) = (f * \eta_\varepsilon)(x) = \int f(y)\,\eta_\varepsilon(x-y)\,dy $$

is a smooth function; $f_\varepsilon \to f$ in $L^p$ for $1\le p<\infty$ as $\varepsilon\to 0$, and uniformly on compacts when $f$ is continuous. This is the standard engine for approximating rough functions by smooth ones — and for proving that $C^\infty_c$ is dense in $L^p$.

Bump explorer & mollification
mollify a rough target with shrinking bumps
ε = 0.20
blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#bump-svg'), out=$('#bump-out');
  const md=$('#bump-mode'), sw=$('#bump-w'), wL=$('#bump-wval');
  const W=640,H=320,pl=40,pr=20,pt=20,pb=40;
  const xmin=-2, xmax=2;
  function bump(x){
    if(Math.abs(x) >= 1) return 0;
    return Math.exp(-1/(1 - x*x));
  }
  // normalize bump to integrate to 1
  let bumpNorm = 0;
  { const N=600; for(let i=0;i<N;i++){ const t=-1+2*i/N; bumpNorm += bump(t)*(2/N); } }
  function moll(x, w){
    // mollifier η_w(x) = w^{-1} * (1/bumpNorm) * bump(x/w)
    if(Math.abs(x) >= w) return 0;
    return (1/w) * (1/bumpNorm) * bump(x/w);
  }
  function rough(x, kind){
    if(kind==='abs') return Math.abs(x);
    if(kind==='step') return x<0 ? 0 : 1;
    if(kind==='saw'){ const t = x - Math.floor(x+0.5); return t; }
    return 0;
  }
  function convolve(x, w, kind){
    // f_w(x) = ∫ f(y) η_w(x-y) dy — numerical quadrature
    const N = 120;
    let s=0;
    for(let i=0;i<=N;i++){
      const y = x - w + 2*w*i/N;
      s += rough(y, kind) * moll(x-y, w) * (2*w/N);
    }
    return s;
  }
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const w = +sw.value; wL.textContent=`ε = ${w.toFixed(2)}`;
    let ymin, ymax, name, kind=null;
    if(md.value==='bump'){ ymin=-0.1; ymax=1.1; name='ψ(x/ε)'; }
    else if(md.value==='mollify-abs'){ ymin=-0.1; ymax=2.1; name='mollify |x|'; kind='abs'; }
    else if(md.value==='mollify-step'){ ymin=-0.2; ymax=1.3; name='mollify step'; kind='step'; }
    else { ymin=-0.7; ymax=0.7; name='mollify sawtooth'; kind='saw'; }
    function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
    function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(0),y1:pt,x2:X(0),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    const M=300;
    if(md.value==='bump'){
      // plot ψ(x/ε)  and η_ε(x) = (1/ε)(1/Z) ψ(x/ε)  normalized to ∫=1
      const pbump=[], pnorm=[];
      for(let i=0;i<=M;i++){
        const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/M;
        pbump.push(`${X(x)},${Y(bump(x/w))}`);
        pnorm.push(`${X(x)},${Y(moll(x,w))}`);
      }
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pbump.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pnorm.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--cyan)','stroke-width':1.5,'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
      const lg=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8, y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
      lg.textContent='ψ(x/ε)'; svg.appendChild(lg);
      const lg2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8, y:pt+30,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--cyan)'});
      lg2.textContent='η_ε(x) normalized'; svg.appendChild(lg2);
      // support markers
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(-w),y1:Y(ymin),x2:X(-w),y2:Y(ymax),stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4','stroke-width':1}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(w),y1:Y(ymin),x2:X(w),y2:Y(ymax),stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-dasharray':'4 4','stroke-width':1}));
      out.textContent =
        `bump  ψ(x) = exp(-1/(1-x²)) on |x|<1\n`+
        `ψ(0) = 1/e ≈ ${Math.exp(-1).toFixed(4)}\n`+
        `support of ψ(x/ε) : [-${w.toFixed(2)}, ${w.toFixed(2)}]\n`+
        `L¹ normalization of bump  :  ∫ψ ≈ ${bumpNorm.toFixed(5)}\n`+
        `mollifier η_ε(x) = (1/ε)/Z · ψ(x/ε) integrates to 1`;
    } else {
      // target (green dashed)
      const tpts=[];
      for(let i=0;i<=M;i++){
        const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/M;
        tpts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(rough(x,kind))}`);
      }
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:tpts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--green)','stroke-width':2,'stroke-dasharray':'5 4'}));
      // mollified (pink)
      const ppts=[];
      for(let i=0;i<=M;i++){
        const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/M;
        const y=convolve(x, w, kind);
        ppts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(y)}`);
      }
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:ppts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':2}));
      const lg=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8, y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--pink)'});
      lg.textContent='f_ε = f ∗ η_ε  (smooth)'; svg.appendChild(lg);
      const lg2=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8, y:pt+30,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--green)'});
      lg2.textContent='rough target f'; svg.appendChild(lg2);
      // sample difference
      const pts = [-0.5, 0, 0.5, 1];
      const errs = pts.map(x0 => Math.abs(convolve(x0,w,kind) - rough(x0,kind)));
      out.textContent =
        `target : ${name}\n`+
        `ε = ${w.toFixed(3)}\n`+
        `f_ε is C^∞ (infinitely differentiable)\n`+
        `|f_ε − f| at sample points ${pts.join(', ')}:\n  ${errs.map(e=>e.toFixed(4)).join(', ')}\n`+
        `errors → 0 as ε → 0 (L^p convergence for 1 ≤ p < ∞).`;
    }
  }
  md.addEventListener('change',render); sw.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Partitions of unity. Given any open cover $\{U_\alpha\}$ of $\mathbb{R}$ (or of a smooth manifold), one can build smooth nonnegative functions $\rho_\alpha$ with $\mathrm{supp}(\rho_\alpha)\subseteq U_\alpha$ and $\sum\rho_\alpha = 1$ pointwise. This lets you patch local constructions into global ones — an indispensable tool for de Rham theory and for integrating forms on manifolds.

14. $C[a,b]$ and the Arzelà–Ascoli theorem

Let $C[a,b]$ be the space of continuous functions $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$, equipped with the sup norm $\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)|$. Under this norm $C[a,b]$ is a Banach space: completeness is exactly the statement that uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (§4). It is not a Hilbert space — the parallelogram law fails for the sup norm.

Arzelà–Ascoli

A family $\mathcal{F}\subseteq C[a,b]$ is pointwise bounded if $\sup_{f\in\mathcal F}|f(x)|<\infty$ for every $x$, and equicontinuous if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is $\delta>0$ (independent of $f$) with $|x-y|<\delta\Rightarrow|f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon$ for all $f\in\mathcal F$.

Arzelà–Ascoli theorem. A set $\mathcal F\subseteq C[a,b]$ has compact closure (equivalently, every sequence in $\mathcal F$ has a uniformly convergent subsequence) if and only if it is pointwise bounded and equicontinuous.

This is the gateway criterion for extracting uniformly convergent subsequences: existence proofs for differential equations (Peano), Montel's theorem on normal families of holomorphic functions, and the existence of minimizers in the calculus of variations all route through Arzelà–Ascoli.

Arzelà–Ascoli: equicontinuous vs not

blocks.script:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg=$('#arz-svg'), out=$('#arz-out');
  const fam=$('#arz-fam'), sn=$('#arz-n'), nL=$('#arz-nval');
  const W=640, H=320, pl=40, pr=20, pt=20, pb=40;
  function render(){
    while(svg.firstChild) svg.removeChild(svg.firstChild);
    const n=+sn.value; nL.textContent=`n = ${n}`;
    let xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax, fk, equicontinuous, familyName;
    if(fam.value==='shift'){
      xmin=0; xmax=2*Math.PI; ymin=-1.2; ymax=1.2;
      fk = (k,x) => Math.sin(x + k*Math.PI/20);
      equicontinuous = true;
      familyName = 'sin(x + kπ/20)';
    } else if(fam.value==='power'){
      xmin=0; xmax=1; ymin=-0.1; ymax=1.1;
      fk = (k,x) => Math.pow(x, 1+1/k);
      equicontinuous = true;
      familyName = 'x^(1+1/k)';
    } else {
      xmin=0; xmax=Math.PI; ymin=-1.2; ymax=1.2;
      fk = (k,x) => Math.sin(k*x);
      equicontinuous = false;
      familyName = 'sin(kx)';
    }
    function X(v){ return pl + (v-xmin)/(xmax-xmin)*(W-pl-pr); }
    function Y(v){ return H-pb - (v-ymin)/(ymax-ymin)*(H-pt-pb); }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pl,y1:Y(0),x2:W-pr,y2:Y(0),stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:X(xmin),y1:pt,x2:X(xmin),y2:H-pb,stroke:'var(--line)'}));
    const colors=['var(--pink)','var(--yellow)','var(--blue)','var(--green)','var(--violet)','var(--cyan)'];
    const M=220;
    let sup=0, modulus=0;
    // probe modulus of continuity at δ = (xmax-xmin)/40
    const delta = (xmax-xmin)/40;
    for(let k=1;k<=n;k++){
      const col = colors[(k-1) % colors.length];
      const pts=[];
      for(let i=0;i<=M;i++){
        const x=xmin+(xmax-xmin)*i/M;
        const y=fk(k,x);
        pts.push(`${X(x)},${Y(y)}`);
        if(Math.abs(y)>sup) sup=Math.abs(y);
      }
      svg.appendChild(SVG('polyline',{points:pts.join(' '),fill:'none',stroke:col,'stroke-width':1.4,'opacity':0.85}));
      // sample |f(x) - f(x+delta)| at several x
      const S=30;
      for(let i=0;i<S;i++){
        const x = xmin + (xmax-xmin-delta)*i/S;
        const diff = Math.abs(fk(k,x+delta) - fk(k,x));
        if(diff > modulus) modulus = diff;
      }
    }
    const lg=SVG('text',{x:W-pr-8,y:pt+14,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    lg.textContent = `family : ${familyName}, k = 1..${n}`; svg.appendChild(lg);
    out.textContent =
      `family     : ${familyName}   ,   ${n} members\n`+
      `sup_{k,x} |f_k(x)|        ≈  ${sup.toFixed(3)}  (pointwise boundedness ✓)\n`+
      `ω(δ) = sup_{k,|x-y|<δ} |f_k(x)-f_k(y)|  at δ = ${delta.toFixed(3)}  ≈  ${modulus.toFixed(3)}\n\n`+
      (equicontinuous
        ? `✓ equicontinuous: ω(δ) → 0 as δ → 0 uniformly in k.\n  By Arzelà–Ascoli, some subsequence converges uniformly on [a,b].`
        : `✗ NOT equicontinuous: for sin(kx), ω(δ) can stay near 2 for arbitrarily small δ (take k ~ π/δ).\n  No uniformly convergent subsequence exists.`);
  }
  fam.addEventListener('change',render); sn.addEventListener('input',render);
  render();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
Why boundedness is needed too. The family $f_n(x) = n$ on $[0,1]$ is equicontinuous (every $f_n$ is constant — $\delta$ can be anything) but not pointwise bounded. No subsequence converges. Both hypotheses of Arzelà–Ascoli are independent and indispensable.
Bridge to functional analysis. $C[a,b]$ is the archetypal function-space Banach space, but the deeper theory lives on Banach and Hilbert spaces, where Hahn–Banach, weak compactness, and Baire-theorem consequences (uniform boundedness, open mapping) extend these ideas dramatically.

</section>
